name: Facility capacity and expansion strategy
consequences: Expanding capacity accelerates recruitment and data collection, enabling earlier milestones. It also increases upfront capital exposure and ongoing operating costs, and raises staffing, space planning, and safety oversight requirements. If expansion outpaces demand or regulatory clearance, utilization may remain suboptimal, undermining cost efficiency.
options: Expand immediately to full 12-suite capacity by accelerating renovations, hiring additional overnight staff, and scaling scheduling systems to support higher throughput.
Maintain the pilot at eight suites while implementing a strict gating framework that ties any expansion to predefined data quality and safety milestones.
Adopt a modular expansion approach using scalable, temporary infrastructure that preserves the domestic environment while enabling phased capacity growth.
review: Core tension centers on rapid scale versus budget, staffing, and safety constraints. A weakness is that none of the options explicitly plans for regulatory delays or community concerns that could stall expansion despite milestone progress.

name: Data governance and privacy posture
consequences: Strengthening privacy and controlled access protects participants and regulatory compliance, but may constrain external validation and collaboration; it may necessitate more formal governance processes and extended timelines for data sharing. This trade-off may slow cross-institution replication but preserves trust and data integrity.
options: Maintain de-identified physiological datasets with access restricted via formal data use agreements and require dual-authenticator access for any external researcher.
Pursue consent-based access to a limited video subset for specialized analyses under strict governance and revocation rights.
Develop on-site data analytics capabilities to minimize external data sharing by processing data locally and only exporting summary statistics.
review: Core tension lies between participant privacy safeguards and the need for external validation and reproducibility. A weakness is that none of the options outlines a concrete approach to mitigate re-identification risk or to harmonize data across sites for multi-study comparisons.

name: Recruitment and retention strategy
consequences: Broadening recruitment and enhancing engagement can speed enrollment and improve sample diversity, aiding longitudinal phenotyping; however, it increases coordination demands and may raise costs, potentially stressing budgets and scheduling.
options: Expand recruitment channels by partnering with regional sleep clinics, neurology networks, and patient advocacy groups to accelerate enrollment and diversify the participant pool.
Enhance participant engagement through flexible scheduling, comprehensive pre-arrival briefings, and ongoing feedback loops to reduce dropout during the extended residential stay.
Refine pre-admission screening to balance safety with recruitment speed by updating collateral guidelines and leveraging centralized collateral history while preserving strict exclusion criteria.
review: Core tension centers on recruitment pace versus safety and budget discipline. A weakness is that the options do not explicitly address potential heterogeneity in data quality arising from a more diverse participant pool or biases in self-reported histories.

name: Financing and governance
consequences: Milestone-based funding aligns spending with progress and reduces upfront risk, but may pressure schedule delivery and create gaps if milestones slip; it could complicate long-term methodological development and delay unfunded innovations.
options: Maintain base funding from DFG and University while establishing a formal milestone-based tranche release to align spend with progress.
Pursue a pharmaceutical industry partnership under the same terms, ensuring publication rights remain with the academic team and defined pre-publication review windows.
Create a dedicated contingency fund and cost-sharing arrangement with university facilities to sustain operations during recruitment lulls and budget variances.
review: Core tension: funding discipline vs research continuity and flexibility. A weakness is that the options do not address potential delays in grant cycles or the risk of misaligned milestones causing underutilization.

name: Operational risk management and safety
consequences: Strengthening safety protocols and staffing coverage reduces incident risk and protects participant welfare, but increases personnel costs and scheduling complexity, potentially slowing expansion pace and utilization efficiency.
options: Institute quarterly independent safety audits and maintain conservative staffing ratios with two overnight technicians per eight suites plus standby responders for emergencies.
Develop a real-time incident response protocol with cross-training between sleep technicians and clinical staff to quickly address safety events and minimize disruptions.
Pilot a comprehensive risk register and escalation framework that triggers pause criteria if incident rates exceed predefined thresholds, allowing swift governance actions.
review: Core tension: safety assurance versus throughput and cost efficiency. A weakness is that none of the options explicitly addresses clinician burnout risk or cross-coverage resilience during extended shifts.

name: Data collection methodology and standardization
consequences: Standardizing data pipelines and ensuring BIDS compatibility enhances data interoperability and reusability across studies, but requires upfront investment in software, training, and quality control, potentially reducing early data collection flexibility.
options: Adopt EEG-BIDS-compliant data workflows across all streams, enforce strict time synchronization, and implement routine quality checks to ensure cross-study compatibility.
Experiment with adaptive sensor configurations to reduce false positives while maintaining data richness, such as calibrating mattress sensors against PSG-derived ground truth on a subset.
Institute a double-rater scoring pipeline with predefined adjudication rules and regular reliability audits to maintain high scoring consistency and traceability.
review: Core tension: data quality and interoperability versus practical flexibility in study protocols. A weakness is that the options do not detail strategies for handling missing data or sensor failures with robust imputation or fallback plans.

name: Ethics, transparency, and stakeholder engagement
consequences: Embedding ethics governance and stakeholder engagement builds trust, consent quality, and regulatory readiness, but may slow protocol iteration and require ongoing resources for governance and community liaison work.
options: Publish annual ethics and methodological updates to the scientific community while maintaining strict privacy controls and consent governance.
Engage patient representatives in protocol reviews to align consent processes with participant perspectives and local regulatory expectations.
Develop a formal data use agreement template with collaborating institutions to streamline multi-site research while preserving data governance.
review: Core tension: ethics governance versus operational agility and speed. A weakness is that the options do not specify how to handle evolving consent for broader future data reuse or participant withdrawal preferences.

name: Phased residential capacity utilization and workflow integration
consequences: Shifting to phased capacity allows scaling up only after achieving data quality targets and establishing safe staffing ratios, reducing upfront capital risk. It creates a stepwise path to full capacity and provides learning opportunities before expansion, but extends the timeline and may delay full data accrual.
options: Adopt a staggered suite activation plan aligned with interim data quality milestones and safety audits to smooth utilization and capex
Prioritize high-utilization nights by scheduling longer participant stays only after achieving consistent low false alarm rates across sensors
Create a flexible staffing model that scales technician coverage with actual event yields, using cross-trained staff to maintain safety while avoiding idle shifts
review: Core tension: balancing space utilization with data quality and safety; a potential rigidity arises if milestones are strictly time-bound, and recruitment delays could stall expansion plans.

name: Sensor integrity, data fusion, and real-time anomaly detection
consequences: Strengthens confidence in multi-modal signals and reduces false positives; improves downstream data analyses and event adjudication efficiency. The trade-off is higher upfront complexity and ongoing maintenance, along with potential integration delays if sensor vendors change specifications.
options: Establish a protocol for routine sensor calibration, cross-validation across modalities, and automated anomaly flags to trigger targeted PSG nights
Invest in a modular data fusion pipeline that supports plug-and-play sensor modules and rapid reconfiguration as study needs evolve
Institute a quarterly external data quality review with an independent statistician to audit sensor alignment and annotation consistency
review: Core tension: data fidelity vs maintenance burden; weakness: heavy calibration schedules may overlook broader vendor risks or supply delays that disrupt the data stream.

name: Participant safety protocol optimization and ethical oversight integration
consequences: Enhances safety through formal escalation procedures and review cycles, improving participant welfare and consent protection. It may slow decision-making and increase governance overhead, potentially affecting recruitment velocity.
options: Institute real-time safety escalation protocols with predefined thresholds and rapid response teams to manage adverse events across nights
Build an ethics liaison role to monitor consent processes, privacy protections, and participant autonomy across all monitoring layers
Develop an adaptive consent process that accommodates ongoing data collection changes while ensuring re-consent when adding new modalities
review: Core tension: safety protocols vs agility; weakness: escalation burden could slow iterative research adjustments and participant onboarding.

name: External collaboration and academic-industrial partnerships framework
consequences: Broadens expertise and funding avenues, accelerating method development and validation; risk of misaligned priorities or governance friction if partner expectations exceed project autonomy.
options: Formalize joint governance with partner institutions to share data access controls, authorship criteria, and milestone-driven collaboration agreements
Establish a selective partner program that brings in third-party data analysts and clinical researchers for defined tasks under clear scope
Create a data-use framework that permits external validation studies while preserving de-identified primary data and consent-laden constraints
review: Core tension: collaboration speed vs governance; weakness: partner alignment risk and potential scope creep that could pressure timelines or data sharing terms.

name: De-identified data curation, repository strategy, and consent-driven data sharing
consequences: Improves reproducibility and external benchmarking while protecting participant privacy; privacy constraints can limit the granularity of some analyses and delayed data release.
options: Implement a tiered data access model with de-identified datasets, controlled researcher access, and repository-level audit trails
Engage participants through dynamic consent mechanisms that allow redefinition of data-sharing preferences over time, with clear revision timelines
Coordinate with institutional repositories to ensure long-term preservation, standardized metadata, and cross-study interoperability for future meta-analyses
review: Core tension: data sharing benefits vs privacy constraints; weakness: de-identification may limit analyses requiring richer contextual data that researchers may seek.

name: Interim analysis governance and progression gates aligned to key milestones
consequences: Provides clear decision points on expansion based on data quality, recruitment progress, and safety outcomes; reduces risk of premature scaling but adds administrative overhead and potential bias if gates are poorly defined.
options: Define explicit progression gates tied to preregistered milestones, ensuring decisions to expand happen only after independent verification of data quality
Institute a quarterly review with external auditors to validate data integrity, recruitment metrics, and protocol adherence before funding reallocations
Publish a quarterly internal update on risk indicators, adjusting timelines and scope in response to observed variances
review: Core tension: speed of decisions vs data maturity; weakness: gates may be gamed or become bottlenecks if thresholds are not robust.

name: Modular capacity expansion linked to pilot data quality milestones
consequences: Expanding only after data quality milestones minimizes wasted capital and ensures infrastructure matches observed study throughput. It also creates a clear trigger for scaling, aligning with staffing plans and regulatory approvals; however, expansion delays may push milestone-based grant targets and publication timelines if milestones prove slower than anticipated.
options: Scale facility capacity in modular phases only after achieving predefined data quality benchmarks and acceptable safety incident rates in the current cohort.
Defer expansion until recruitment-to-event capture ratios stabilize across three consecutive cohorts, ensuring staffing, annotation throughput, and equipment utilization remain within target spans.
Pilot the fully integrated 12-suite configuration in parallel with a dedicated risk reserve, enabling rapid expansion if early data indicate scalable success while containing cost.
review: Core tension: speed of expansion vs readiness of clinical and data infrastructure. Weakness: milestone-driven triggers may overlook funding cycles or partner commitments if timelines slip.

name: Sensor deployment rigor and calibration cadence
consequences: Enforcing calibration and cross-validation improves data fidelity and reduces false alarms, strengthening downstream analyses and confidence in findings. The trade-offs include higher maintenance overhead, scheduling complexity for PSG nights, and increased nightly data review workload for technicians.
options: Institute a rigorous calibration calendar across all sensors, with cross-validation against PSG nights to minimize false positives and improve data integrity.
Adopt adaptive sensor prioritization, phasing out lower fidelity devices in favor of higher-resolution modalities only after pilot phase demonstrated utility.
Invest in automated anomaly detection software and manual review integration to balance real-time monitoring with expert validation, preserving data quality while limiting reviewer fatigue.
review: Core tension: data fidelity versus maintenance burden. Weakness: calibration cadence may not translate into better downstream outcomes if data processing lags or device downtime disrupts schedules.

name: Participant intake protocol tightening and pre-screen risk stratification
consequences: Enhancing pre-screening improves yield and safety margins by filtering ineligible or high-risk participants early; downstream, it may bias the cohort toward clearly defined cases and increase pre-admission administrative workload.
options: Implement a multi-tier pre-screen process with collateral history and structured exclusions to improve yield and safety margins before admission.
Introduce adaptive inclusion criteria based on interim safety assessments to optimize event yield while protecting participants.
Hybrid intake using remote screening tools and in-person assessments to broaden reach without compromising data quality.
review: Core tension: screening efficiency vs cohort representativeness. Weakness: stringent pre-screening could exclude relevant subgroups and limit generalizability.

name: Staffing model redesign for nocturnal coverage and safety margins
consequences: Shaping staffing around redundancy raises safety and data throughput, reduces fatigue-related errors, and improves adherence to protocols. The trade-offs include higher labor costs and scheduling complexity, with potential difficulty recruiting specialized staff for night shifts.
options: Reconfigure night-shift staffing with overlapping teams to maintain redundancy, reduce fatigue, and improve incident response.
Cross-train technicians for data annotation and safety procedures to improve throughput without increasing headcount significantly.
Establish situation-based staffing triggers tied to event rates, enabling lean operations during quieter weeks while protecting safety.
review: Core tension: safety margins vs staffing cost. Weakness: overlapping shifts and cross-training may dilute specialized roles and raise onboarding burden.

name: Video data privacy and governance negotiations with ethics boards
consequences: Privacy-preserving governance reduces risk of data breaches and permits broader collaborations, but may constrain data sharing timelines and limit external validation if access controls are too restrictive.
options: Negotiate de-identification protocols and access gates with ethics committees to enable broader external collaborations while maintaining privacy.
Limit video storage duration and implement strict access controls with logging to reduce exposure and potential data misuse.
Pilot a restricted video sampling framework that supports method development without public access, then evaluate expansion based on results.
review: Core tension: privacy constraints vs collaboration opportunities. Weakness: too restrictive governance can impede external validation and multi-site studies.

name: Event triage tooling integration and human-in-the-loop workflow
consequences: Integrating semi-automatic event triage tools onto manual scoring accelerates data processing and scalability of analyses. However, there is a risk of algorithmic bias and drift without ongoing validation, requiring robust IT governance and periodic recalibration.
options: Layer semi-automated event triage tools onto manual scoring, reducing reviewer burden while preserving adjudication accuracy.
Implement periodic calibration sessions between raters and algorithm outputs to tighten inter-rater reliability and align scoring criteria.
Pilot a tiered alert system that flags high-likelihood events for expedited review while routing ambiguous cases to consensus scoring.
review: Core tension: automation versus human judgment. Weakness: over-reliance on triage tools may bias case review if not regularly recalibrated.
